summary: Organized in Harlem on August 25, 1925 under A. Philip Randolph, the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters became the first predominantly African American labor union chartered by the American Federation of Labor and the first to win a collective bargaining agreement with a major U.S. corporation, the Pullman Company, in 1937.
locationLabel: Harlem, New York City (union organizing center)

claims.object: A. Philip Randolph and Pullman porters founded the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters in New York City on August 25, 1925 as the first predominantly African American labor union in the United States
claims.citationLabel: A. Philip Randolph, Labor & Civil Rights Activist Born — Library of Congress

claims.object: In September 1940 Eleanor Roosevelt addressed the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters convention at the Harlem YMCA, where Randolph and porter delegates pressed for defense-industry jobs and desegregation of the armed forces
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: A. Philip Randolph

historicalContext: Twelve years of organizing preceded the Pullman Company's 1937 recognition of the BSCP; the union's Harlem base linked porters' shop-floor grievances to national campaigns against employment discrimination that Randolph later carried into the March on Washington Movement and the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

summary: More than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven national unions met at Chicago's LaSalle Hotel on September 23–24, 1972 to form the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists, creating a permanent caucus to advance Black workers' interests inside the AFL-CIO.

claims.object: On September 23–24, 1972, more than 1,200 Black union officials and rank-and-file members from thirty-seven international and national unions met at the LaSalle Hotel in Chicago and established the Coalition of Black Trade Unionists as a permanent organization
claims.citationLabel: CBTU History

claims.object: Five Black labor leaders — William Lucy, Nelson Edwards, William Simons, Charles Hayes, and Cleveland Robinson — convened the founding conference after the AFL-CIO executive council took a neutral stance in the 1972 presidential election
claims.citationLabel: The Coalition of Black Trade Unionists is Formed

historicalContext: CBTU emerged when Black trade unionists concluded that AFL-CIO neutrality in 1972 would not protect Black workers from Nixon-era retrenchment; the Chicago meeting remains the largest single gathering of Black unionists in U.S. labor history and the group continues as an AFL-CIO constituency organization.

summary: Black labor leaders convened the founding convention of the Negro American Labor Council at Detroit's Statler-Hilton Hotel on May 27–29, 1960, electing A. Philip Randolph president to press the AFL-CIO to end segregated locals and elect Black workers to union leadership.

claims.object: Dissatisfied with AFL-CIO President George Meany's civil-rights record, A. Philip Randolph and other African American labor leaders formed the Negro American Labor Council in 1960 to carry out the federation's civil-rights program from a Black workers' organization
claims.citationSource: The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute
claims.citationLabel: Negro American Labor Council (NALC)

claims.object: The NALC founding convention met May 27–29, 1960 at the Statler-Hilton Hotel in Detroit, electing Randolph president and Cleveland Robinson of District 65 as vice president
claims.citationLabel: NALC Founding Convention Program, Statler-Hilton Hotel, Detroit, 1960

historicalContext: Randolph's NALC bridged the Brotherhood of Sleeping Car Porters' labor tradition and the civil-rights movement; its 1961 recommendations to the AFL-CIO demanded desegregated locals, Black staff hires, and Black representation on policy-making bodies, and Randolph later chaired the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom.

summary: In December 1869, 214 delegates met in Washington, D.C. to form the Colored National Labor Union as a counterpart to the white National Labor Union, petitioning Congress for land redistribution and credit for Black farmers excluded from most trade unions.

claims.object: In December 1869, 214 delegates attended the Colored National Labor Union convention in Washington, D.C., forming a national organization because white trade unions generally excluded Black workers
claims.citationLabel: African Americans and the American Labor Movement

claims.object: The 1869 convention petitioned Congress to subdivide public lands in the South into forty-acre farms and provide low-interest loans to Black farmers, and sent a further memorial in January 1871 on conditions in the southern states
claims.citationLabel: National Park Service: 200 Years of Labor History

historicalContext: Isaac Myers, a Baltimore ship-caulker who had organized the Colored Caulkers Trade Union Society, served as the CNLU's president; though the union lasted only a few years, it documented Reconstruction-era Black workers' demand for both industrial organization and agrarian reform.

summary: After garbage collectors Echol Cole and Robert Walker were crushed in a malfunctioning truck on February 1, 1968, roughly 1,300 mostly Black Memphis sanitation workers struck for sixty-five days, demanding AFSCME recognition, safer equipment, and wages above poverty levels.
locationLabel: Memphis, Tennessee (city hall and public-works strike center)

claims.object: On February 12, 1968, about 1,300 Black sanitation workers employed by the Memphis Department of Public Works walked off the job after the deaths of Echol Cole and Robert Walker, demanding union recognition, safer trucks, and higher pay
claims.citationSource: The Martin Luther King, Jr. Research and Education Institute
claims.citationLabel: Memphis Sanitation Workers' Strike

claims.object: After a silent march of an estimated 42,000 supporters on April 8 and continued negotiations, the strike ended April 16, 1968 with city recognition of AFSCME Local 1733, wage increases, and a non-discrimination clause
claims.citationLabel: Memphis Sanitation Workers Went on Strike — Library of Congress

historicalContext: Strikers carried "I AM A MAN" placards that linked dignity to economic rights; Martin Luther King Jr. joined the cause as part of the Poor People's Campaign before his assassination in Memphis on April 4, 1968, and the settlement became a landmark public-sector organizing victory.

summary: Black autoworkers at Chrysler's Dodge Main plant in Hamtramck, Michigan formed the Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement in May 1968 after a wildcat strike over speed-ups and racial job segregation, publishing the DRUM newsletter to challenge both Chrysler management and UAW Local 3 leadership.
locationLabel: Hamtramck, Michigan (Chrysler Dodge Main plant area)

claims.object: The Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement was formed in May 1968 at the Chrysler Dodge Main plant in Hamtramck, Michigan, among activists including General Baker, Mike Hamlin, John Watson, and Kenneth Cockrel
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records

claims.object: On May 2, 1968, DRUM staged a wildcat strike at Dodge Main demanding improvements for Black workers; a July 8, 1968 stoppage drew an estimated 4,000 workers out of the plant
claims.citationLabel: Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement — 12th Street Detroit

historicalContext: DRUM pioneered the Revolutionary Union Movement model — plant-based Black caucuses that used wildcat strikes and shop-floor newspapers when official unions would not address racial hierarchies on the assembly line; it became a core member of the League of Revolutionary Black Workers in 1969.

summary: Formed in Detroit in 1969, the League of Revolutionary Black Workers united Dodge Revolutionary Union Movement chapters and related plant caucuses into a federation that coordinated wildcat strikes, propaganda, and challenges to UAW leadership over systemic racism in auto plants.

claims.object: In 1969 the League of Revolutionary Black Workers was created as the central organization for Revolutionary Union Movement groups formed after the 1968 DRUM wildcat strike at Dodge Main
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records — Reuther Library

claims.object: Factional infighting caused the League to split in 1971 and its influence subsequently declined, though its records document Black autoworkers' demands during the peak of the Black Power era
claims.citationLabel: Detroit Revolutionary Movements Records — finding aid

historicalContext: The League extended the DRUM model to Eldon Avenue Gear and Lighting (ELRUM), Ford's River Rouge complex (FRUM), and non-auto workplaces including UPS and Detroit newspapers, forcing the UAW and Chrysler to confront Black workers' charges that union bureaucracy mirrored shop-floor racism.

summary: Farmworkers in Shaw, Mississippi voted in April 1965 to form the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union and strike the Seligmann plantation, registering roughly one thousand members within two weeks and demanding a $1.25 minimum wage, an eight-hour day, and employer-paid medical coverage.

claims.object: In April 1965, forty-five Shaw farmworkers voted unanimously to found the Mississippi Freedom Labor Union with a drafted constitution and economic demands, then struck the Seligmann plantation
claims.citationLabel: Freedom to Resist: John Henry Sylvester and Strike City, Mississippi

claims.object: Within two weeks of the Shaw vote the union registered about one thousand members and more than two hundred workers were on strike across the Delta, calling for a $1.25 minimum wage, an eight-hour workday, and free medical coverage
claims.citationLabel: Project South: Freedom Labor Union (FLU)

historicalContext: The MFLU grew from Freedom School conversations and support from the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and Delta Ministry, extending civil-rights organizing into plantation labor after the 1964 Civil Rights Act; strikers later established Strike City near Cleveland, Mississippi when evicted from plantation housing.

summary: Black Workers for Justice formed in 1981 after Black women Kmart employees in Rocky Mount, North Carolina organized a boycott against race and gender discrimination; activists from ten counties met in Fremont in June 1982 to launch a statewide Black workers' organization.
locationLabel: Rocky Mount, North Carolina (founding struggle city)

claims.object: Black Workers for Justice formed in 1981 out of a struggle led by Black women workers at a Kmart store in Rocky Mount, North Carolina against race and gender discrimination
claims.citationLabel: About Us — Black Workers for Justice

claims.object: After a local Kmart boycott, Black workers and community activists from ten counties met at First Missionary Baptist Church in Fremont, North Carolina in June 1982 to establish BWFJ as a statewide organization
claims.citationLabel: Acting Locally and Globally: The Black Workers for Justice

historicalContext: Operating from a Workers Center in Rocky Mount, BWFJ later supported Schlage Lock plant workers fighting closure, families after the 1991 Imperial Foods fire in Hamlet, and the formation of UE Local 150 for North Carolina public-sector workers.

summary: ILA Local 1422 in Charleston, South Carolina represents a waterfront workforce with deep roots in post-emancipation Black longshore organizing; the local gained national attention during the 2000 Charleston Five dispute over use of non-union labor at the port.

claims.object: ILA Local 1422 in Charleston represents longshore workers on the city's docks; the Avery Research Center documents a lineage from the 1869 Longshoremen's Protective Union Association of Black stevedores to the ILA local restored in 1936
claims.citationLabel: Longshoreman Strike 1866 — Avery Research Center

claims.object: Five Local 1422 members known as the Charleston Five faced felony riot charges after a January 19, 2000 confrontation on the docks over the Danish shipping company Nordana's use of non-union workers
claims.citationSource: College of Charleston Avery Research Center
claims.citationLabel: Charleston Five Collection

historicalContext: Charleston's Black longshoremen organized strikes for higher wages within months of emancipation and built durable union institutions through Reconstruction and Jim Crow; Local 1422 leaders including Kenneth Riley Jr. carried that waterfront tradition into twenty-first-century global solidarity campaigns.

summary: In the summer of 1881, Black washerwomen in Atlanta's Summer Hill neighborhood formed the Washing Society and struck for a uniform rate of one dollar per dozen washes, growing to roughly three thousand members and threatening a general strike that forced concessions from the city's commercial elite.
locationLabel: Atlanta, Georgia (Summer Hill washerwomen organizing district)

claims.object: In summer 1881, Black washerwomen in Atlanta's Summer Hill district formed the Washing Society, set a standard rate of one dollar per dozen washes, and coordinated chapters across the city that grew to nearly three thousand members
claims.citationSource: UC Santa Barbara Undergraduate Journal of History
claims.citationLabel: The 1881 Atlanta Washerwoman Strike in the Pages of the Atlanta Constitution

claims.object: The washerwomen's coordinated action in 1881 grew so large that organizers threatened a general strike that would have shut Atlanta down, forcing the city's business and political establishment to negotiate
claims.citationLabel: Atlanta's Washerwomen Strike — Virginia State University

historicalContext: Less than two decades after emancipation, Atlanta's washerwomen used church networks and neighborhood committees to wage one of the most organized labor campaigns in the post-Reconstruction South, demonstrating how Black women domestic workers could translate collective power into wage standards.

displayName: Federation of Southern Cooperatives/Land Assistance Fund
summary: Established in 1967 to support Black farmers and rural communities across the South, the Federation of Southern Cooperatives opened its Rural Training and Research Center near Epes, Alabama in 1971 on land purchased with the Panola Land Buying Association, training members in cooperative management and land retention.
locationLabel: Rural Training and Research Center, Epes, Alabama

claims.object: The Federation's Rural Training and Research Center in Epes, Alabama opened in 1971 on a 374-acre tract purchased jointly with the Panola Land Buying Association to train low-income rural farmers in cooperative and farming practices
claims.citationLabel: FSC/LAF Rural Training and Research Center records

claims.object: Formed out of the civil rights movement, the Federation of Southern Cooperatives was established in 1967 to create community-based economic development for Black farmers and rural communities across the South
claims.citationLabel: CPCRS: Federation of Southern Cooperatives Rural Training & Research Center

historicalContext: The Epes center served member cooperatives in more than a dozen states with workshops on cattle, greenhouse, and row-crop production while credit unions and revolving loan funds helped families resist the land loss that accelerated after the 1960s; the site is under study for National Register listing.

summary: Incorporated in April 1966 in Alberta, Alabama near Gee's Bend, the Freedom Quilting Bee was a women's craft cooperative that grew from the civil rights movement to market quilts nationwide, with Estelle Witherspoon as president and more than sixty quilters at its first meeting in March 1966.
locationLabel: Alberta, Alabama (cooperative headquarters near Gee's Bend)

claims.object: The Freedom Quilting Bee cooperative was incorporated in April 1966 in Alberta, Alabama with Estelle Witherspoon as president after more than sixty quilters attended the group's first meeting in March 1966
claims.citationLabel: The Freedom Quilting Bee — Southern Changes

claims.object: Founded in 1966 out of the civil rights movement, the Bee created economic opportunities for African American women in Wilcox County by manufacturing and selling quilts nationwide
claims.citationLabel: Auburn University: Freedom Quilting Bee

historicalContext: Rev. Francis X. Walter's interfaith civil-rights work in west Alabama linked quilters from Gee's Bend and Alberta to New York buyers; the cooperative gave Black Belt women income and organizational experience during voting-rights and land-rights struggles in one of the poorest counties in the United States.

summary: Founded in New York City in 1932 as a pharmacists' union, Local 1199 expanded in the late 1950s into voluntary hospitals where a heavily African American and Puerto Rican workforce waged landmark strikes; Martin Luther King Jr. called 1199 his favorite union before it merged into SEIU in 1998.
locationLabel: Montefiore Medical Center area, Bronx, New York (early hospital organizing)

claims.object: After organizing Montefiore Hospital in the late 1950s, Local 1199 launched large-scale campaigns among voluntary New York hospitals where a large proportion of workers were Black or Puerto Rican, including a forty-six-day strike beginning May 8, 1959
claims.citationLabel: Building a Union in the Hospitals: Local 1199 at Montefiore

claims.object: Martin Luther King Jr. famously described Local 1199 as his favorite union, and Coretta Scott King later served as honorary chair of the union's organizing campaigns as 1199 expanded beyond New York
claims.citationLabel: 1199SEIU United Healthcare Workers East — NYU Finding Aid

historicalContext: 1199's hospital campaigns fused postwar civil-rights mobilization with industrial unionism in New York's voluntary hospitals; the union's 1930s Harlem pickets against segregated drugstores preceded decades of Black and Latina worker leadership in healthcare organizing across the Northeast.

summary: At the 2007 U.S. Social Forum in Atlanta, more than fifty domestic workers from thirteen grassroots organizations voted to form the National Domestic Workers Alliance, creating a national body to advance dignity, standards, and organizing for nannies, house cleaners, and home care workers.
locationLabel: Atlanta, Georgia (2007 U.S. Social Forum founding site)

claims.object: The National Domestic Workers Alliance was founded at the U.S. Social Forum in Atlanta, Georgia in 2007 when domestic workers from thirteen organizations representing five states voted to form a national alliance
claims.citationLabel: Founded — National Domestic Workers Alliance

claims.object: Founding member organizations included Domestic Workers United and other New York, California, Maryland, and Bay Area groups that had spent years building local domestic-worker campaigns before the Atlanta gathering
claims.citationLabel: About NDWA

historicalContext: NDWA extended a lineage from Dorothy Bolden's 1960s Atlanta domestic-worker organizing through state bill-of-rights campaigns in New York, California, and elsewhere; the Atlanta founding linked local worker centers into a national voice for a workforce historically excluded from federal labor protections.
